summary: Gruber traces his lifelong fascination with dots and pixels, from Pac-Man arcade screens through dot-matrix printers to laser output, establishing that smaller dots have always meant better quality. He reviews the 15-inch MacBook Pro with Retina Display as a revolutionary leap in pixel density, arguing it finally bridges the gap between screen and print quality that has frustrated designers for decades. He notes that sub-pixel anti-aliasing, once thought to become superfluous at high resolutions, actually becomes even more valuable on retina displays. He concludes that the retina display has permanently altered his perception, making all non-retina screens feel like crude approximations.
key_points: The history of computing displays has been a slow march from ~72 PPI to ~130 PPI over nearly 30 years, but the retina MacBook Pro's 220 PPI represents a sudden, dramatic leap forward
Sub-pixel anti-aliasing, contrary to earlier predictions, becomes more valuable rather than superfluous at retina resolutions — delivering pure benefit with no visible trade-offs
Fonts optimized for screen rendering look cheap on retina displays, just as they do in print — high-resolution screens demand fonts designed for high-resolution output
Lucida Grande, Mac OS X's system font since 2001, is now exposed as a low-resolution-optimized font that should be replaced, with Gruber betting on Myriad as a longshot successor
The retina display inverts perception: rather than retina feeling special, everything non-retina now feels ersatz and approximate
money_quotes: I can think of almost nothing I have done professionally over the last 20 years that has not been rendered as a matrix of dots.
When the individual dots are indistinguishably small, the illusion is complete.
We loved our Macs and loved designing with them, but what we saw on-screen wasn't our design work. It was a crude pixelated approximation of our design work.
Like no Apple device since the original 2007 iPhone, the new Retina 15-inch MacBook Pro feels like a device from the near future, something slightly beyond the ken of today's cutting edge.
I can only tell you how profound the difference is. I can't show you.
To say that text is rendered at print quality is to imply that you have one hell of a professional-grade printer.
Fonts optimized for screen rendering look cheap on the retina MacBook Pro — sometimes downright cheesy — in the same way they do when printed in a glossy magazine.
Now, only the retina MacBook Pro feels real to me, and all my other Macs feel ersatz.
key_insight: The retina MacBook Pro doesn't just improve display quality — it permanently inverts your perception so that all non-retina screens feel like crude approximations of what computing should look like.
